paper_title: Ice Core Analysis and Bipolar Seesaw Model Testing

methods.method_name: Ice Core Sample Preparation and BeO Formation
methods.description: This method describes the preparation of ice core samples for isotope analysis, specifically focusing on the formation of BeO for AMS measurements. The procedure is modified to handle smaller sample sizes compared to previous studies.

steps.description: The ice available for this study was in the form of a continuous series of 55 cm strips, each weighing ∼300 g. In order to maximize the time resolution, each bag was divided into 5 samples of 11 cm in length, each representing ∼9 years.
steps.searchable_summary: divide ice core strips into smaller samples for higher time resolution

steps.description: The samples were melted in 250 ml centrifuge cones, in the presence of 0.25 mg of 9 Be carrier.
steps.searchable_summary: melt ice samples with Be carrier in centrifuge cones

steps.description: The Be(OH)2 was then precipitated directly with NH4OH.
The precipitate was then washed with water (pH=7), dissolved in a few drops of nitric acid, and transferred to a quartz crucible.
steps.searchable_summary: wash and dissolve Be(OH)2 precipitate, transfer to quartz crucible

steps.description: The crucible was taken to dryness on a hotplate, then heated to 900°C over an electric furnace in order to transform the precipitate to BeO.
The BeO was mixed with Nb powder (325 mesh) in the ratio 3:1 and pressed into a 1 mm diameter, 1 mm deep hole in a Mo cathode.
steps.searchable_summary: mix BeO with Nb powder and press into Mo cathode

steps.description: This cathode was heated in argon to 1200°C for 1 min, then 1800°C for an additional minute, in a resistively heated carbon furnace.

methods.description: This method involves measuring the 10Be/9Be ratios using AMS and analyzing the uncertainties associated with the measurements.

steps.description: The 10 Be/ 9 Be ratios were measured at the Gif-sur-Yvette Tandetron based AMS facility, relative to NIST standard SRM 4325, using the certified ratio of 2.68×10 −11.

For almost all samples, at least 200 Be events were recorded, leading to a 1 sigma statistical uncertainty of <7%. Combined with a conservatively estimated 5% machine uncertainty, this leads to an overall uncertainty of ∼8.5%.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate statistical and machine uncertainty for Be measurements

methods.method_name: Testing the Thermal Bipolar Seesaw Model
methods.description: This method tests the thermal bipolar seesaw model using new synchronization based on 10Be and temperature reconstructions from ice cores.

steps.description: We now test the thermal bipolar seesaw concept with the new synchronization based on 10 Be.
steps.searchable_summary: test bipolar seesaw model with new 10Be synchronization

steps.description: The new temperature reconstructions based on thermal fractionation measured on air enclosed in samples from the NorthGRIP ice core is used as input to the seesaw model with τ =1200 and 2200 years.
steps.searchable_summary: use temperature reconstructions as input to seesaw model

steps.description: The output is compared to the temperature record at Dome C inferred from the δD signal.
steps.searchable_summary: compare model output to Dome C temperature record
